The vendor's Assembly Procedure shall specify the correct method for the make-up of midbody connections, such as a torque value, make-up position, torque-turns, torque position, or torque shape to ensure proper make-up. See section 7.30 for the required shop makeup procedure.

## 7.24.4 Testing Requirements

The Testing Procedure developed by the vendor for frac sleeves shall include the following items:

### 7.24.4.1 Functional Testing

All necessary testing parameters, including the frequency of testing, shall be included in the vendor's Testing Procedure.

### 7.24.4.2 Testing Apparatus

The testing apparatus shall allow for pressure testing and access from above. The test fixture shall allow for pressure testing of the frac sleeve, access for the activating mechanism(s), and circulation throughout the test. In addition, since frac sleeves are often used in horizontal or nearly horizontal hole sections, the test fixture shall replicate actual field conditions as accurately as possible, including its orientation.

## 7.24.5 Post-Qualification Requirements

Clean and dry the entire frac sleeve, including the threaded end connections. Apply thread compound and thread protectors to the end connections. Place a 2 inch wide (±1/4 inch) white paint band around an accepted component. The paint band should be 6 inches ±1 inch from the pin shoulder. The paint band should be 6 inches ±2 inches from the box shoulder for box by box components. Using a permanent paint maker on the outer surface of the tool, write or stencil the applicable DS-1 qualification class, the date, and the name of the company that performed the inspection.

## 7.25 Visual API Round Connection Inspection

### 7.25.1 Scope

This procedure covers visual examination of new and used API round connections typically found on completions equipment that are compatible with tubing connections to evaluate the conditions of the connections.

### 7.25.2 Inspection Apparatus

a. A 12-inch metal ruler graduated in 1/64 inch increments, a calibrated pit depth gauge, a calibrated API round thread profile gauge, an OD caliper, and a calibrated white light intensity meter to verify illumination are required.
b. See section 1.7 for calibration requirements for the white light intensity meter, pit depth gauge, and profile gauge.

### 7.25.3 Preparation

a. Connections shall be clean so that no scale, mud, or lubricant can be wiped from the thread or face surfaces with a clean rag.

b. The full-height thread length (minimum length of full-crest threads), LC, of the pin connection, included in Figure 7.55, shall be measured using a metal ruler from the nose of the pin connection to the distance in Table 7.54 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from non-upset tubing, Table 7.55 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from externally upset tubing, or Table 7.56 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from integral tubing. The root of the thread closest to the measured LC but not at a length less than LC shall be identified. This will be used as a reference point for the visual API round connection inspection procedure.
c. The effective thread length, L2, of the pin connection, included in Figure 7.55, shall be measured using a metal ruler from the nose of the pin connection to the distance in Table 7.54 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from non-upset tubing, Table 7.55 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from externally upset tubing, or Table 7.56 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from integral tubing. The root of the thread closest to the measured L2 but not at a length less than L2 shall be identified. This will be used as a reference point for the visual API round connection inspection procedure.
d. The minimum illumination level at the inspection surface shall be 50 foot-candles. The white light intensity level at the inspection surface shall be verified:

- At the start of each inspection;
- When light fixtures change positions or intensity;
- Where there is a change in relative position of the inspected surface with respect to the light fixture;

![img-68.jpeg](img-68.jpeg)
Figure 7.55 Thread dimensions of an API round pin connection.

- When requested by the customer or a designated representative; and
- Upon completion of the inspection.

These requirements do not apply to direct sunlight conditions. If adjustments are required to the light intensity level at the inspection surface or if the light intensity is found to be less than 50 foot-candles when measured upon completion of the inspection, all components inspected since the most recent light intensity level verification shall be re-inspected.

### 7.25.4 Procedure and Acceptance Criteria

a. Cracks: Any cracks shall be cause for rejection for new and used pin and box connections. Grinding to remove cracks is not permitted.

b. Total Length of a Box Connection: The total length of a box connection, included in Figure 7.56, shall be measured from the box face to the last scratch farthest from the box face using the metal ruler. This measurement shall be recorded as the box length and shall meet requirements from Table 7.57 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from non-upset tubing, Table 7.58 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from externally upset tubing, or Table 7.59 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from integral tubing, or the connection shall be rejected.

c. Perfect Thread Length (PTL): The Perfect Thread Length (PTL) of the box connection shall be determined by subtracting Reference Dimension A, shown in Figure 7.56, from the actual box length that was recorded in accordance with paragraph 7.25.4b. The value for Reference Dimension A is included in Table 7.57 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from non-upset tubing, Table 7.58 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from externally upset tubing, or Table 7.59 for a box connection compatible with a pin from integral tubing. This mathematically determined PTL shall be measured from the face of the box connection using the metal ruler. The root of the thread that is closest to the mathematically determined PTL but not at a length less than the PTL shall be identified. This will be used as a reference point for the visual API round connection inspection procedure.

d. Total Length, L4: The total length, L4, of a new or used pin connection, included in Figure 7.55, shall be measured from the pin nose using the metal ruler and shall meet requirements from Table 7.54 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from non-upset tubing, Table 7.55 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from externally upset tubing, or Table 7.56 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from integral tubing, or the connection shall be rejected.

e. Full-Height Threads of New Connections: All threads between the nose of a new pin connection and the designated thread root at LC, except the thread closest to the pin nose, shall have full crests or the connection shall be rejected. All threads between the face of a new box connection and the designated thread root at the PTL, except the thread closest to the box face, shall have full crests or the connection shall be rejected. The thread profile gauge shall mesh with the thread loading and stabbing flanks so that no light is visible at any of the flanks or thread roots. Four thread profile

![img-69.jpeg](img-69.jpeg)
Figure 7.56: Thread dimensions of an API round box connection.

checks 90 degrees ±10 degrees apart shall be made on each connection.

f. Full-Height Threads of Used Connections: All threads between the nose of a used pin connection and the designated thread root at LC, except the thread closest to the pin nose, shall have full crests or the connection shall be rejected. All threads between the face of a used box connection and the designated thread root at the PTL, except the thread closest to the box face, shall have full crests or the connection shall be rejected. The thread profile gauge shall be used as a reference to check for major imperfections to the threads, such as raised metal, torn threads, pulled threads, or severely sharpened threads. Examples of acceptable and rejectable thread conditions are shown in Figures 7.57–7.59. Four thread profile checks 90 degrees ±10 degrees apart shall be made.

g. Damages to Threads of New Connections: All threads (including those within L4 on a pin connection and every thread on a box connection) shall be free of all pitting, or the connection shall be rejected. All threads shall be free of raised metal, torn threads, pulled threads, galling, missing threads, and other imperfections, or the connection shall be rejected. Raised protrusions shall be removed with a hand file or "soft" (nonmetallic) buffing wheel.

h. Damages to Threads of Used Connections: All threads (including those within L4 on a pin connection and every thread on a box connection) shall be free of all pitting in the thread roots or the connection shall be rejected. Threads between the pin nose and the designated thread root at LC or between the box face and the designated thread root at the PTL shall be free of raised metal, torn threads, pulled threads, or severely sharpened threads, or the connection shall be rejected. Thread surfaces within L2 on a pin connection and all threads on a box connection shall be free of imperfections, including pits, that appear to either penetrate below the thread root, occupy more than 3/4 inch in length along any thread helix, or exceed 1/32 inch in depth or 1/16 inch in diameter. Raised protrusions must be removed with a hand file or "soft" (nonmetallic) buffing wheel. The thread profile gauge shall be checked after any buffing or cleaning of the threads using the thread profile gauge. Examples of acceptable and rejectable thread conditions are shown in Figures 7.57–7.62.

i. Box Connection Dimensions of New and Used Connections: The box connection OD shall be

![img-70.jpeg](img-70.jpeg)
Figure 7.57 Acceptable thread condition.

![img-71.jpeg](img-71.jpeg)
Figure 7.58 Rejectable thread condition due to deformed threads.

![img-72.jpeg](img-72.jpeg)
Figure 7.59 Rejectable thread condition due to sharpened threads.

![img-73.jpeg](img-73.jpeg)
Figure 7.60 Rejectable thread condition due to pitting that exceeds 1/16 inch in diameter.

![img-74.jpeg](img-74.jpeg)
Figure 7.61 Rejectable thread condition due to imperfections that occupy more than 3/4 inch in length along a thread helix.

![img-75.jpeg](img-75.jpeg)
Figure 7.62 Rejectable thread condition due to pitting that exceeds 1/32 inch in depth.

measured for new and used connections in two locations at least $90 \pm 10$ degrees apart using the OD calipers and the metal ruler. This measurement shall be recorded and shall meet vendor requirements or, if applicable, Table 7.60 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from non-upset tubing or externally upset tubing or Table 7.61 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from integral tubing. A straightedge shall be placed along the longitudinal axis of the box connection OD. If a visible gap exists between the straightedge and the box connection, the OD shall also be measured in the location with the gap using the OD calipers and the metal ruler.

j. OD Imperfections of New and Used Box Connections: The depth of any pits, gouges, grip marks, or other imperfections on the OD of a new or used box connection shall be measured using the pit depth gauge. The vendor shall specify acceptance criteria for the maximum depth of any imperfections. If the vendor does not have these acceptance criteria specified, then the box connection shall be rejected if the imperfection has a depth greater than the maximum depth included in Table 7.60 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from non-upset tubing or externally upset tubing or Table 7.61 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from integral tubing. If a gouge has an adjacent metal protrusion, then the protrusion shall be removed prior to making a depth measurement.

k. Pin Nose Chamfer: A pin nose chamfer not present for a full (360 degree) circumference is cause for rejection. A thread root that runs out on the pin nose, a feather edge, or a knife edge (razor edge) is cause for rejection. Examples of these scenarios can be found in Figure 7.63, while a properly machined pin nose chamfer can be seen in Figure 7.55.

![img-76.jpeg](img-76.jpeg)
Figure 7.63 Rejectable features of an API (8-round) tubing pin nose.

1. Dents: Dents or mashes that cause out-of-tolerance thread dimensions are cause for rejection. Any dents on the nose of a new pin connection or face of a new box connection shall be cause for rejection. Any dents on the nose of a used pin connection or face of a used box connection shall be measured using the pit depth gauge. The depth of the imperfection shall not exceed 1/8 inch for a used connection, or the connection shall be rejected.

## 7.26 Dimensional API Round Connection Inspection

### 7.26.1 Scope

This procedure covers dimensional examination of new and used API round connections typically found on completions equipment that are compatible with tubing connections to evaluate the condition of the connections.

### 7.26.2 Inspection Apparatus

a. A calibrated lead gauge and calibrated lead setting standards are required. The accuracy of the lead gauge shall be verified by applying the gauge to the appropriate lead setting standard. Prior to adjusting the lead gauge, the gauge ball diameter (shown in Figure 7.64) shall be checked with a calibrated micrometer or caliper with precision to the thousandths and shall be:

- 0.072 inch ±0.002 inch for 8-round threads, or
- 0.057 inch ±0.002 inch for 10-round threads.

See section 1.7 for calibration requirements for the gauge and each setting standard as well as the micrometer or caliper.

b. Both a calibrated external thread taper gauge and a calibrated internal thread taper gauge are required. The gauge ball diameter (shown in Figure 7.64) of the taper gauges shall be checked with a micrometer or caliper with precision to the thousandths and shall be:

![img-77.jpeg](img-77.jpeg)
Figure 7.64 Example of a gauge ball diameter.

- 0.072 inch ±0.002 inch for 8-round threads, or
- 0.057 inch ±0.002 inch for 10-round threads.

See section 1.7 for calibration requirements for the gauge as well as the micrometer or caliper.

c. A calibrated thread height gauge is required. Either a balanced-dial type gauge or a continuous-reading type gauge shall be used. If a balanced-dial type gauge is used, it shall be placed on a calibrated setting standard with the gauge ball within the notch and be set so that the dial registers zero. If a continuous-reading type gauge is used, it too shall be placed on a setting standard with the gauge ball within the notch so that the dial registers the appropriate thread height of:

- 0.071 inch ±0.001 inch for 8-round threads; or
- 0.056 inch ±0.001 inch for 10-round threads.

It is also acceptable to place the gauge ball of the continuous-reading type gauge on a flat machined surface so that the dial registers zero. See section 1.7 for calibration requirements for the gauge and the standard.

d. Both a calibrated external thread pitch diameter gauge and a calibrated internal thread pitch diameter gauge are required to measure pitch diameters unless this is done using taper gauges. The pitch diameter gauges shall be zeroed using appropriate calibrated standards, such as rod standards or frame standards. See section 1.7 for calibration requirements.

e. Each gauge shall be standardized:

- At the start of each inspection;
- After inspecting 50 connections with that gauge; and
- Upon completion of the inspection.

If upon completion of the inspection the most recent standardization accuracy cannot be verified, then all connections inspected since the most recent standardization shall be re-inspected.

f. A calibrated white light intensity meter shall be used to verify illumination. See section 1.7 for calibration requirements.

### 7.26.3 Preparation

a. Connections shall be clean so that no scale, mud, or lubricant can be wiped from the threads or any other surface with a clean rag.

# 7.9.5 Function Testing Requirements

The Function Test Procedure developed by the vendor for safety valves, kelly valves, and inside blowout preventers (IBOPs) must include a shell hydrotest and differential hydrotest per the requirements listed below.

## 7.9.5.1 Precaution, Preparation, and Equipment Requirements

Before applying hydrotest pressure, make sure that the part to be tested is isolated behind a barricade of sufficient size and strength to prevent injury in the event of a leak or shell rupture. Make sure all air is bled from the test system before applying pressure. Follow all other safety procedures established for the facility.

a. Block and bleed required. After applying pressure to the test piece in all of the tests below, the pressure source shall be isolated and the line pressure between the source and the test piece shall be bled to zero. Hydrotest time periods do not begin until these steps are accomplished.

b. Acceptance. The minimum pressure hold period for each test is 5 minutes. Any observable drop in pressure or water leakage through a valve body or around the valve operating stem, ball, or plug during the test period shall be cause for rejection of the valve.

c. Gage calibration. See section 1.7 for calibration requirements for pressure gages used for hydrotesting. Pressure measuring devices shall be calibrated to maintain $\pm 2\%$ accuracy of full-scale range. Pressure gage must be able to read at least $150\%$ of the test pressure but not more than $500\%$ of the test pressure.

## 7.9.5.2 Differential Test from Below

(Applicable to kelly valves, safety valves, and IBOPs)

a. Attach the lower (pin) end of the valve to the test fixture. The valve shall be in the closed position with no cap or plug on the upper (box) end.

b. Apply 200 psig from the lower (pin) end of the valve. Block and bleed the pressure source. Hold pressure for 5 minutes while examining the ball, operating stem, and midbody connection for leaks. Observe the pressure gage for pressure drop.

c. After the valve successfully passes the test at 200 psig, repeat the test as above at the full rated pressure of the valve.

d. Bleed pressure to zero.

## 7.9.5.3 Differential Test from Above

(Applicable to bidirectional kelly valves only)

a. Attach the upper (box) end of the valve to the test fixture. The valve shall be in the closed position with no cap or plug on the lower (pin) end.

b. Apply 200 psig from the upper (box) end of the valve. Block and bleed the pressure source. Hold pressure for 5 minutes while examining the ball, operating stem and midbody connection for leaks. Observe the pressure gage for pressure drop.

c. After the valve successfully passes the test at 200 psig, repeat the test as above at 5000 psig, or the full rated pressure of the valve (whichever is lower).

d. Bleed pressure to zero.

## 7.9.5.4 Shell Test

(Applicable to kelly valves, safety valves, and IBOPs)

a. Attach the lower (pin) end of the valve to the test fixture. The valve shall be in the open position with another valve, a cap or plug on the upper (box) end.

b. Apply the rated working pressure from the lower (pin) end of the valve. Block and bleed the pressure source. Hold pressure for 5 minutes, and examine the ball, operating stem, and midbody connection for leaks. Observe the pressure gage for pressure drop.

c. On kelly valves and safety valves, while maintaining test pressure, cycle the ball open and closed at least three times while observing the operating stem for leaks. No leaks are permitted. Leave valve in the open position. Bleed pressure to zero.

## 7.9.6 Post-Qualification Requirements

a. Clean and dry the threaded connections. Visually inspect thread and seal surfaces in accordance with paragraphs 7.14.5.2 and 7.14.5.4 to ensure damage did not occur from the test plugs during hydrostatic pressure test. Apply appropriate thread compound. Install thread protectors.

b. Attach a parts bag or similar to the valve. The parts bag shall contain at least one operating wrench (for safety valves and kelly valves) or opening tool (for inside BOPs). For tools so equipped, the parts bag shall also contain an allen wrench for removing the plug from the grease fitting hole and at least two grease fittings.

b. The full-height thread length (minimum length of full-crest threads), LC, of the pin connection, included in Figure 7.55, shall be measured using a metal ruler from the nose of the pin connection to the distance in Table 7.54 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from non-upset tubing, Table 7.55 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from externally upset tubing, or Table 7.56 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from integral tubing. The root of the thread closest to the measured LC but not at a length less than LC shall be identified. This will be used as a reference point for the dimensional API round connection inspection procedure.

c. The effective thread length, L2, of the pin connection, included in Figure 7.55, shall be measured using a metal ruler from the nose of the pin connection to the distance in Table 7.54 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from non-upset tubing, Table 7.55 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from externally upset tubing, or Table 7.56 for a pin connection compatible with a box connection from integral tubing. The root of the thread closest to the measured L2 but not at a length less than L2 shall be identified. This will be used as a reference point for the dimensional API round connection inspection procedure.

d. Total Length of a Box Connection: The total length of a box connection, included in Figure 7.56, shall be measured from the box face to the last scratch farthest from the box face using the metal ruler. This measurement shall be recorded as the box length and shall meet requirements From Table 7.57 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from non-upset tubing, Table 7.58 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from externally upset tubing, or Table 7.59 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from integral tubing, or the connection shall be rejected.

e. Perfect Thread Length (PTL): The Perfect Thread Length (PTL) of the box connection shall be determined by subtracting Reference Dimension A, shown in Figure 7.56, from the actual box length that was recorded in accordance with Paragraph 7.26.3.d. The value for Reference Dimension A is included in Table 7.57 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from non-upset tubing, Table 7.58 for a box connection compatible with a pin connection from externally upset tubing, or Table 7.59 for a box connection compatible with a pin from integral tubing. This mathematically determined PTL shall be measured from the face of the box connection using the metal ruler. The root of the thread that is closest to the mathematically determined PTL but not at a length less than the PTL shall be identified. This will be used as a reference point for the visual API round connection inspection procedure.

f. If the nominal OD of the tubing with which the connection is compatible is at least 2-7/8 inches, the connection shall be divided into 1 inch intervals starting with the full-height thread root closest to the nose of a pin connection or face of a box connection. The final inspection interval shall coincide with the designated thread root at LC for a pin connection or at the PTL for a box connection, or an overlapping interval shall be provided. An overlapping interval is provided by starting at the designated thread root at LC or at the PTL and identifying thread roots towards the pin nose or box face until the new interval overlaps the previously designated interval.

g. If the nominal OD of the tubing with which the connection is compatible is less than 2-7/8 inches, the connection shall be divided into 1/2 inch intervals starting with the full-height thread root closest to the nose of a pin connection or face of a box connection. The final inspection interval shall coincide with the designated thread root at LC for a pin connection or at the PTL for a box connection, or an overlapping interval shall be provided. An overlapping interval is provided by starting at the designated thread root at LC or at the PTL and identifying thread roots towards the pin nose or box face until the new interval overlaps the previously designated interval.

h. The minimum illumination level at the inspection surface shall be 50 foot-candles. The white light intensity level at the inspection surface must be verified:

- At the start of each inspection;
- When light fixtures change positions or intensity;
- Where there is a change in relative position of the inspected surface with respect to the light fixture;
- When requested by the customer or a designated representative;
- Upon completion of the inspection.

These requirements do not apply to direct sunlight conditions. If adjustments are required to the light intensity level at the inspection surface or the light intensity is found to be less than 50 foot-candles when measured upon completion of the inspection, all components inspected since the most recent light intensity level verification shall be re-inspected.

## 7.26.4 Procedure and Acceptance Criteria

a. Thread Lead: The thread lead shall be measured using a lead gauge. Only threads between the pin nose and the designated thread root at L2 for a pin connection or between the box face and the designated thread root at the PTL for a box connection shall be subjected to a lead measurement. Lead measurements shall be made starting at the full-height thread closest to the nose of a pin connection or face of a box connection and continued at 1 inch intervals if the nominal OD of the tubing with which the connection is compatible is at least 2-7/8 inches or 1/2 inch intervals if the nominal OD of the tubing with which the connection is compatible is less than 2-7/8 inches until the full-height thread farthest from the pin nose or box face is measured and the overlap interval is measured (if applicable). If a seal-ring groove is present in a box connection, lead measurements shall be taken with all gauge balls placed where the full thread forms are on each side of the gauge balls. The partial threads adjacent to the groove shall be avoided when measuring lead. The thread lead shall be:

- ±0.003 inch per inch for 1 inch intervals on new and used connections; and
- ±0.0015 inch per inch for 1/2 inch intervals on new and used connections.

b. Pin Connection Taper: The external thread taper gauge shall be used to measure the taper of a pin connection. The movable gauge ball shall be diametrically opposite the fixed gauge ball. The gauge shall be applied to the full-height thread closest to the nose of the pin connection with the fixed gauge ball held firmly within the thread root while the movable gauge ball is oscillated through an arc within the thread root. The dial pointer should indicate zero at the maximum reading and shall be adjusted if the reading is not zero. The gauge shall be applied at 1 inch intervals past the full-height thread closest to the nose of the pin connection if the nominal OD of the tubing with which the connection is compatible is at least 2-7/8 inches or 1/2 inch intervals if the nominal OD of the tubing with which the connection is compatible is less than 2-7/8 inches until the full-height thread farthest from the pin nose is measured and the overlap interval is measured (if applicable). The taper shall be:

- 0.063 inch per inch, + 0.005 inch per inch, - 0.003 inch per inch for new connections; and
- 0.063 inch per inch, + 0.005 inch per inch, - 0.005 inch per inch for used connections.

c. Box Connection Taper: The internal thread taper gauge shall be used to measure the taper of a box connection. The gauge shall be placed with the fixed gauge ball on the fifth thread from the center for 8-round connections and on the sixth thread from the center for 10-round connections. The movable contact shall be placed on the bottom thread that is diametrically opposite the fixed gauge ball. The dial pointer should indicate zero at this position as the movable gauge ball is oscillated through an arc within the thread root. The gauge shall be applied at 1 inch intervals past the starting thread if the nominal OD of the tubing with which the connection is compatible is at least 2-7/8 inches or 1/2 inch intervals past the starting thread if the nominal OD of the tubing with which the connection is compatible is less than 2-7/8 inches from the original location until the full-height thread farthest from the box face is measured. The taper shall be 0.063 inch per inch, + 0.005 inch per inch, - 0.003 inch per inch for new and used connections.

d. Thread Height: The thread height gauge shall be used to measure the thread height of all threads between the pin nose and the designated thread root at LC, except the thread closest to the pin nose, or between the box face and the designated thread root at the PTL, except the thread closest to the box face. The tip of the thread height gauge shall be placed in the thread root with the anvil of the gauge resting on the crest of the thread. The anvil shall be aligned with the axis of the threads by oscillating the gauge through an arc about the longitudinal axis of the anvil. The thread height is indicated correctly when the dial indicator stops moving near the center of the arc. The thread height shall be:

- 0.071 inch, +0.002 inch, -0.004 inch for 8-round threads on new connections;
- 0.056 inch, +0.002 inch, -0.004 inch for 10 round threads on new connections;

115

- 0.071 inch, +0.004 inch, -0.004 inch for 8-round threads on used connections; and
- 0.056 inch, +0.004 inch, -0.004 inch for 10 round threads on used connections.

e. Pitch Diameter and Ovality: For a connection compatible with tubing that has a nominal OD of at least 2-3/8 inches, an external thread pitch diameter gauge or an external thread taper gauge shall be used to measure the pitch diameter and ovality of a pin connection, while an internal thread pitch diameter gauge or an internal thread taper gauge shall be used with a box connection. A connection compatible with tubing that has a nominal OD of less than 2-3/8 inches shall not be measured for pitch diameter or ovality. If taper gauges are used, procedures (but not acceptance criteria) from API Technical Report 5TRSR22 shall be applied. If pitch diameter gauges are used, the external thread pitch diameter gauge shall be placed squarely against the nose of the pin connection with the pivot shoes on the crests of the threads, and the internal thread pitch diameter gauge shall be placed squarely against the face of the box connection. Using the lower shoe as a pivot, the pitch diameter gauge shall be oscillated through an arc to identify the largest and smallest readings. The pitch diameter gauge shall then be turned 90 degrees ±10 degrees, ensuring 360 degree coverage of the connection, to identify the largest and smallest readings. The average pitch diameter variation and ovality shall be calculated as follows and shall meet requirements in Table 7.53 regardless of which gauges are used to measure the dimensions:

- Average pitch diameter variation = (largest reading + smallest reading) / 2
- Ovality = largest reading – smallest reading

## 7.27 Drift Testing

### 7.27.1 Scope

This procedure covers the drifting of a component to detect any reduction in the ID.

### 7.27.2 Inspection Apparatus

a. A drift mandrel shall be used to perform drift testing.
b. See section 1.7 for calibration requirements for any electronic, dial, or Vernier device used to verify the OD of the drift mandrel. The measuring device shall also have flat contacts and be capable of measuring to a precision of 0.001 inch.

### 7.27.3 Preparation

a. The vendor of the component shall provide a drifting procedure that includes requirements for the OD of the drift mandrel, the length of the drift mandrel, the material that comprises the drift mandrel, and the depth to which the drift mandrel shall be inserted into the ID of the component. In addition, the following procedures are mandatory.
b. The drift mandrel shall be thoroughly clean and appropriate for use. The drift mandrel shall also be serialized with a unique identification number. It shall be visually inspected for any damages. The drift mandrel must have a constant OD and be continuous throughout its specified length. Disk and barbell-shaped mandrels shall not be used. The ends of the drift mandrel extending beyond the specified cylindrical portion shall be shaped to permit easy entry into the ID of the component.
c. When measuring dimensions of the drift mandrel, the mandrel and the measuring device used shall be within 10 degrees Fahrenheit of each other after being stabilized for at least 30 minutes to ensure accurate measurement.
d. The length of the cylindrical (measuring) portion of the drift mandrel shall be explicitly stated in the vendor's inspection procedures. The length of the drift cylinder shall be measured to the nearest 1/8 inch.
e. The OD of the drift mandrel shall be inspected at a minimum of four (4) locations: within 1 inch of both ends of the drift, within 2 inches of the middle of the drift and 4 inches from the end that is most likely to wear. At least two measurements at each location shall be obtained 90 degrees apart (for a minimum of 8 measurements) to a precision of 0.001 inch. The allowable minimum drift cylinder diameter shall be explicitly stated in the vendor's inspection procedures.
f. The drift mandrel OD shall be verified at minimum:

- At the start of each work order;
- After drifting 500 components;
- At the end of every shift;
- When requested by the customer or a designated representative;

- At the end of each work order.
g. The drift mandrel and the component shall be within 10 degrees Fahrenheit of each other when the drift enters the ID of the component.

## 7.27.4 Procedure

Pass the drift mandrel through the entire length of each joint of tubing. The drift mandrel shall pass through the ID of the tubing freely using a reasonably-exerted force that does not exceed the weight of the drift mandrel. The drift should be inserted and removed carefully so that the threads are not damaged.

## 7.27.5 Acceptance Criteria

If the drift mandrel does not pass through the entire length of tubing, remove and clean the drift mandrel. Attempt the drift test again starting from the other end of the joint of tubing. If the drift mandrel does not pass through the entire length of the tubing on the second attempt, the component shall be rejected.

## 7.28 Ultrasonic Wall Thickness Inspection

### 7.28.1 Scope

This procedure covers the ultrasonic measurement of wall thickness using compression waves.

### 7.28.2 Inspection Apparatus and Calibration

a. The ultrasonic instrument shall be the pulse-echo type with a digital display.
b. The transducer shall have separate transmitting and receiving elements. Any transducer worn to a degree that light is visible under its face when placed on the reference standard without couplant shall be made flush or replaced.
c. Linearity Calibration: The instrument shall be calibrated for linearity over an interval of 0.100 inch to 2.000 inches after any repair of the instrument or at least every six months. See section 1.7 for calibration requirements.
d. The same type of couplant shall be used for both calibration and gauging.
e. The field reference standard shall be of the same acoustic properties as the component being inspected

and shall have at least two thicknesses that meet the following requirements:

- Thick Section = Nominal wall +0.100, -0.000 inch
- Thin Section = Nominal wall +0.000, -0.100 inch

f. The field reference standard shall be verified to be within ±0.002 inch of the stated thickness by a calibrated electronic, dial, or Vernier device. See section 1.7 for calibration requirements for this verifying device.
g. After field calibration adjustments, the gauge shall measure both thicknesses on the standard to an accuracy of ±0.001 inch.
h. Field calibration shall be verified at the following frequency:

- At the start of each inspection
- After each 50 inspected components
- When a measurement indicates a rejectable component
- Each time the unit is turned on
- When the gauge is suspected of having been damaged
- When probe, cable, operator, or tubing size or weight are changed
- Upon completion of each inspection

i. If the previous field calibration accuracy cannot be verified, all areas tested since the last valid calibration shall be re-gauged after correcting the calibration.

### 7.28.3 Preparation

a. All components shall be sequentially numbered.
b. The external surface where the readings are to be taken shall be cleaned to the bare surface.

### 7.28.4 Procedure

a. The parting line between the transmitting and receiving elements on the two-element transducer shall be held perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the component.
b. The inspector shall scan the surface within a 1-inch radius of the lowest reading to confirm or modify that value. The lowest reading shall be recorded.

# 7.29 Specific Requirements for Shop Maintenance of Air Hammer Tools

## 7.29.1 Scope

This section provides additional specific requirements for shop inspection, assembly, and function testing of air hammers.

## 7.29.2 Preparation

The following steps must be performed to prepare for the inspection, assembly, and function testing of air hammers.

### 7.29.2.1

Record the tool serial number and tool description. Reject the tool if no serial number can be located unless the customer waives this requirement.

### 7.29.2.2

When qualifying the components to the maintenance classification A1, disassemble the tool completely, breaking all midbody connections, removing all inserts, plugs, seats, seals, and springs.

## 7.29.3 Inspection Requirements

The Inspection Program developed by the vendor for inspection of air hammers must include the following procedural requirements and common inspection methods.

### 7.29.3.1 Apparatus

The following equipment must be available for inspection: paint marker, pit gage, a light capable of illuminating the entire internal surface, metal scale, tape measure, flat file or disk grinder, and an inspection mirror. All apparatus requirements specified in the applicable common inspection methods are also required.

### 7.29.3.2 Common Inspection Methods Required

- Sub Inspection (7.12)
- Visual Connection Inspection (7.14)
- Dimensional 3 Inspection (7.16)
- Blacklight Connection Inspection (7.17)
- MPI Body Inspection (7.19)

### 7.29.3.3 Reference Diagram

To assist the inspector, Figure 7.65 diagrams an example air hammer assembly labeled with the component nomenclature referenced within this standard.

### 7.29.3.4 Visual Body and Internal Hardware Inspection

Examine all outside surfaces including but not limited to the case, chuck, top sub, piston, bit, and other components for mechanical damage. A cut, gouge, erosion, corrosion, cavitation, or similar flaw shall be cause for rejection of the component if the flaw:

a. Is deeper than 10% of the adjacent wall for tubular components such as the case and chuck.

b. Is deeper than 10% of the component thickness for odd-shaped components such as the piston and bit retainer. Thickness for odd-shaped components is defined as the smallest distance between opposite surfaces, measured at the thinnest point (see Figure 7.4).

c. Is found on any internal or external bearing surface, anvil face, or sealing surface including but not limited to the outer sealing surface of the piston, the anvil face of the piston or bit shank, the internal bearing surface of the inner cylinder or case, or the splines on the chuck or bit shank. If a new bit shank is used, a new chuck shall also be used.

![img-78.jpeg](img-78.jpeg)
Figure 7.65 Example air hammer assembly.

d. Exceeds the limits given in the vendor's Inspection Procedure for the tool in question. In cases where the flaw size exceeds the limits in a. through c. above, but does not exceed the specific limits allowed in the vendor's Inspection Procedure, or no flaw size limitation is listed in the vendor's Inspection Procedure, the vendor's engineering department may further evaluate and propose acceptance of the flawed component, provided it does so in writing with reference to the specific flaw(s) in question. If the vendor's engineering department evaluates and accepts the flaw in writing and is agreed upon by the customer, the component shall be accepted and the written acceptance shall become part of the inspection report to the customer. Otherwise, the part must be rejected.

## 7.29.3.5 MPI Body Inspection Coverage

When performing the MPI Body Inspection (section 7.19) the inspection shall cover the outside surface of all components including but not limited to the case from shoulder to shoulder, the piston, the chuck, and bit shank. Recheck all components except the tool structural case with the magnetic field oriented perpendicular to the first (longitudinal) scan.

## 7.29.3.6 Ring Gauge Inspection

Check the bit diameter by using "go" and "no-go" gauges. For accurate measurement, the temperature of the "go" and "no-go" gauges shall be within 20 degrees Fahrenheit of the bit shank after being stabilized for 30 minutes. Both the "go" and "no-go" gauges shall be fabricated from steel 1 inch in height with an OD equal to the nominal bit size plus 1-1/2 inch. The ID of the "go" gauge shall equal the nominal bit size plus 0.002 in clearance with a tolerance of +0.003 inch, 0 inch. The ID of the "no-go" gauge shall be equal to the minimum bit size (nominal size less maximum negative tolerance) minus 0.002 inch interference with a tolerance of 0 inch, -0.003 inch. The "go" gauge shall be carefully slid over the over the length of the bit with a force which does not exceed the weight of the ring gage. The inside diameter of the ring gage shall be verified using internal micrometer specified in paragraph 7.8.2.1. For acceptance, the "go" gage shall pass smoothly over the bit and inserts and bit and inserts shall not pass through "no-go" gauge.

## 7.29.4 Assembly Requirements

The Assembly Procedure developed by the vendor for air hammers must include the following items:

## 7.29.4.1 Seals

Replace old O-rings and soft seals with new ones prior to reassembly.

## 7.29.4.2 Incidental Components

Visually inspect all incidental components such as springs, washers, and the like for wear and damage. Any components with evidence of deformation, washing, or cracking shall be replaced.

## 7.29.4.3 Blow Tube

If a blow tube is present, it shall be pressed into place and not hammered. After seating it shall be inspected for damage. If the blow tube is found to be damaged it shall be replaced.

## 7.29.4.4 Oiling Requirements

All internal surfaces must be sufficiently oiled prior to assembly, with the same type of oil to be used on the rig.

## 7.29.4.5 Connection Make-Up

The Assembly Procedure shall specify the connection torques for midbody connections as well as placement locations for the tongs of the torqueing tool. As such, the torqueing tongs shall be placed at the location specified in the Assembly Procedure and made up to the torque numbers specified in the Assembly Procedure. See section 7.30 for required shop makeup procedure.

## 7.29.4.6 Air Flow Setup Guidelines

Guidelines for airflow setup including but not limited to check valve selection, adjustment, or shimming, choke sizing, and hydrocyclone configuration shall be provided in the Assembly Procedure. The airflow setup must be traceable to the drilling parameters associated with the expected application.

## 7.29.5 Function Testing Requirements

The Function Test Procedure developed by the vendor for air hammers must include the following items:

## 7.29.5.1 Test Configurations

Function tests shall be performed in both the off-bottom (blowing but not firing) and on-bottom (firing) configurations. The air hammers shall be tested within the specified pressure ranges given in the tool operating manual for both orientations. During the off-bottom test, the tool shall continuously blow and not fire the hammer mechanism for at least 10 seconds; any reciprocation of

the hammer mechanism is cause for rejection. During the on-bottom test, the tool shall fire the hammer mechanism continuously for at least 10 seconds; any interruption in the reciprocation of the hammer mechanism shall be cause for rejection. Throughout the entire test, the air hammer shall remain in a vertical position. Testing equipment shall show evidence of calibration in accordance with the equipment manufacturer's requirements or annual calibration, whichever is more stringent.

## 7.29.5.2 Gage Calibration

Pressure measuring devices shall be calibrated to maintain $\pm 2\%$ accuracy of full-scale range. Pressure gauge must be able to read at least $150\%$ of the test pressure but not more than $500\%$ of the test pressure. See section 1.7 for calibration requirements for the pressure and airflow gages used for function testing.

## 7.29.6 Post-Qualification Requirements

Clean and dry the threaded connections. Visually inspect thread and seal surfaces in accordance with paragraphs 7.14.5.2 and 7.14.5.7 to ensure damage did not occur from the test plugs during function test. Apply appropriate thread compound. Install thread protectors. Place a 2-inch wide ($\pm 1/4$ inch) white paint band around an acceptable component. The paint band should be 6 inches $\pm 1$ inch from the pin shoulder. The paint band should be 12 inches $\pm 2$ inches from the box shoulder for box by box components. Using a permanent paint marker on the outer surface of the tool, write or stencil applicable DS-1 qualification class, the date, and the name of the company performing the inspection.

## 7.29.7 Storage Requirements

- Before storing an air hammer ensure it has been thoroughly oiled.
- After oiling, both ends of the air hammer shall be covered to keep the internal components clean and moisture free.
- Air hammers shall be stored and transported in a horizontal orientation to prevent oil starvation.
- Air hammers shall be used within 90 days or returned to a shop for disassembly, inspection, and rebuild.

## 7.30 Shop Makeup Procedure

### 7.30.1 Scope

This procedure covers the proper application of optimal makeup torques (MUT) and verification of bucked on assemblies shipped to rigs to ensure connections are made up correctly.

### 7.30.2 Determining Optimal MUT

a. Drilling and Completion Applications: The MUT applied to API Rotary-Shouldered Connections (RSCs) to be used for drilling applications shall be the API recommended MUT as defined in API RP 7G unless specifically requested otherwise by the customer. API MUT values can be calculated using various programs, including T H Hill Associates' Drillstring Toolbox™ app. Some bevel diameters specified by DS-1 Volume 1 may result in seal stresses that exceed the yield strength of the material when API MUT is applied (see paragraph 7.30.2g).

b. Landing Applications: The MUT applied to API RSCs to be used for landing applications shall be based on the lower of API MUT or T4 values as defined in API RP 7G. T4 MUT values provide the maximum tensile capacity without shoulder separation. T4 MUTs can be calculated using various programs, including T H Hill Associates' Drillstring Toolbox. Some bevel diameters specified by DS-1 Volume 1 may result in seal stresses that exceed the SMYS when T4 MUT is applied (see paragraph 7.30.2g).

c. Proprietary Connections: MUTs and makeup requirements for proprietary connections shall be provided by the manufacturer. DS-1 Volume 2 contains several tables of recommended makeup torque values, but these must be verified with the manufacturer's recommendations. When used in a landing string application, the equipment provider must verify that the required tensile capacity is fit for purpose.

d. OCTG Connections: MUTs applied to proprietary OCTG connections shall be obtained from the manufacturer and applied with continuous-turn equipment using the torque-turn method. If required by the customer, a licensed thread representative may be present when making up proprietary connections. API OCTG connections shall be made up to the API nominal power-tight position.

e. Fit-for-Purpose MUTs: In the event where a MUT to be applied does not meet the criteria listed above, a fit-for-purpose technical justification must be provided. The written technical justification should at a minimum include the tensile and torsional capacity as well as the estimated tensile and torsional loads

to prove the connection's properties are suitable. This technical justification shall be included in the equipment's Assembly Check Sheet.

f. Other Threads: MUTs applied to connections not covered above (ACME threads, proprietary, etc) shall be obtained from the thread manufacturer.

g. MUT and Bevel Diameter: When possible, bevel diameters should be selected to optimize seal stresses (i.e. remain below a max of 100% of specified minimum yield strength, SMYS). If existing bevel diameters are not optimal, then fit-for-purpose MUTs should be used to ensure seal stresses do not exceed the SMYS of the connections while still providing the needed tensile and torsional capacity (which must be confirmed with the customer). These torque values and their justifications must be included in the Assembly Check Sheet.

## 7.30.3 Preparation

a. Calibration: See section 1.7 for calibration requirements for torque application equipment.

b. MUT Traceability: All MUT charts shall be labeled with the description and serial number of the made up components. Charts and graphs should be included in the Assembly Check Sheet.

c. Make and Break (M&amp;B): Breaking in newly re-cut connections using M&amp;B is recommended but not required. If a tool provider elects to perform M&amp;B, it should be performed at least 3 times at the recommended MUT. The connections should be made up slowly to avoid galling the threads. After the M&amp;B is completed (3 cycles), the connections shall be visually examined. M&amp;B at times may be cost prohibitive and involve extra handling risks. Consideration should be given to this before proceeding.

d. Cross Torqueing: Cross torqueing is not permitted. Cross torqueing is the action of putting components in a torque machine with multiple connections needing applied torque, and simultaneously transmitting torque across all connections. Cross torqueing is only permitted when vendor specifications require a cross torque.

## 7.30.4 Procedure

a. The MUT shall be verified prior to making up the connections as described in section 7.30.2. The basis for determining the MUT includes the connection type, box OD, pin ID, stress relief features, SMYS of material, and application. These connection properties as well as the MUT applied shall be recorded on the Assembly Check Sheet.

b. Thread compound shall be applied in a thin, even layer to all contacting surfaces (threads, shoulders, seals, etc) unless the manufacturer's recommendations state otherwise. Generally thread lock compounds should be applied to the thread surfaces but not seal surfaces; typical thread compound should be applied to the seal surfaces. In any case, the vendor shall follow the manufacturer's recommendations for coverage.

c. The friction factor of the thread compound used shall be recorded. Unless the vendor specification requires otherwise, the MUT applied shall be adjusted by the thread-compound friction factor (MUT used = Standard MUT × FF).

d. For any connections on which thread-locking compound is applied, the final torque values should be adjusted by multiplying by the corresponding friction factor. The friction factor and expiration date of the thread locking compound should be checked prior to application. This information is usually indicated in the packaging of the thread locking compound. If the information is unavailable, the thread locking compound should not be used except when the information is provided in the form of a Certificate of Conformance (COC) by the compound manufacturer.

e. Optional (may be required if specified by the customer): On shouldered connections that are made up hand tight prior to applying torque, an initial scribe line may be marked across the connection with a paint marker before applying the final MUT. After the MUT is applied, the scribe line should be offset. The MUT should be noted with a paint marker next to the offset scribe lines. Note: It is acceptable if these markings are later scraped off or covered during the final tool preparation.

f. On all connection types, once the final MUT has been applied and accepted per the applicable procedures, a torque stripe approximately 1 inch wide × 18 inches long shall be painted across the tight connection. The MUT shall be stenciled or marked near the painted torque stripe as follows: "Torqued to XXXX ft-lb." The torque stripe and MUT shall be clearly visible on the tool, after all preparations, so it can be verified on the rig.

Photographs in this section provided by Chevron.

![img-79.jpeg](img-79.jpeg)
Figure 7.66 Initial scribe line.

![img-80.jpeg](img-80.jpeg)
Figure 7.67 Offset scribe line and MUT.

![img-81.jpeg](img-81.jpeg)
Figure 7.68 Final torque stripe.

![img-82.jpeg](img-82.jpeg)
Figure 7.69 Not torqued connections.

g. Documentation, including bucking equipment calibration records, torque graphs, and torque-turn and torque-time graphs shall be included in the Assembly Check Sheet.

h. Occasionally, due to operational needs, connections must be shipped to the rig that are not torqued up. When this occurs, the words “LOOSE - NOT TORQUED” must be clearly marked or stenciled across the connection on two sides, 180 degrees apart. The rig should always be made aware of the operational need to ship the connection loose. The shipping documentation should also reflect the loose connection.

## 7.31 Specific Requirements for Shop Maintenance of Elevator Links

### 7.31.1 Scope

This section provides additional specific requirements for the shop inspection of elevator links.

### 7.31.2 Preparation

The following steps must be performed to prepare for the inspection of the elevator links:

#### 7.31.2.1 Verification of Customer Requirements

Determine from the customer the required equipment tensile ratings and elevator link lengths. If any of the equipment properties do not meet the customer requirements, do not proceed with the inspection; notify the customer.

#### 7.31.2.2 Preparation of Surfaces for Inspection

a. Critical area maps for each component shall be provided by the vendor to the inspector so that the proper inspection areas can be prepared for inspection. If critical area maps are not provided by the vendor, the entire tool shall be treated as critical and inspected on 100% of its area.

b. All foreign material such as paints, coatings, dirt, grease, oil, scale, etc shall be removed from the critical areas prior to inspection. A form of grit-blasting is the preferred method for preparing equipment surfaces for inspection. However, other methods capable of completely removing the foreign material from the

critical areas can be used, such as wire brush wheels, soft buffing pads, steam-cleaning, and paint-stripping.

## 7.31.3 Inspection Requirements

The Inspection Program developed by the vendor for inspection of elevator links must include the following procedural requirements and common inspection methods:

### 7.31.3.1 Apparatus

The following equipment must be available for inspection: paint marker, metal scale, tape measure, spring caliper. All apparatus requirements specified in the applicable common inspection methods are also required.

### 7.31.3.2 Traceability

Record the elevator links pair serial number and tool description. Reject the components if no serial number can be located unless customer waives this requirement.

### 7.31.3.3 Common Inspection Methods Required

- MPI Body Inspection (7.19) with coverage as identified in 7.31.3.6.
- Liquid Penetrant Inspection (7.18) if necessary for proving up indications found using MPI Body Inspection.

### 7.31.3.4 Visual Inspection

Visually inspect the surfaces of the components to ensure no damages are present in the manufacturer-defined critical areas. If damages are present, evaluate according to the acceptance criteria provided by the manufacturer. Elevator links shall be visually inspected for excessive wear on the shank and eyes. Unacceptable wear (as defined by the manufacturer's acceptance criteria) on a critical area is cause for rejection.

### 7.31.3.5 Dimensional Inspection

In addition to the vendor's specified dimensional inspections, the following dimensional wear checks are required for shop qualification of elevator links. Dimensions listed below shall be measured, recorded, and compared to the manufacturer's allowable tolerances. If any dimension requires de-rating of the equipment, the tool shall be rejected unless waived by the customer.

a. Upper and lower eye thickness of each link.

b. Shank diameter (minimum diameter along the shank length) of each link.
c. Overall length from contact points of the eyes. Link sets up to and including 14 feet in length (as measured between the contact surfaces) shall match within 1/8 inch. Links over 14 feet in length shall match within 1/4 inch.

### 7.31.3.6 MPI Body Inspection Coverage

When performing the MPI Body Inspection (section 7.19) common inspection method, the inspection shall cover all of the critical areas for each load bearing component as defined by the manufacturer's critical area maps. If no critical area maps are available, the entire component shall be considered critical and inspected over its entire area. Recheck all critical areas with the magnetic field oriented perpendicular to the first field.

### 7.31.3.7 Acceptance Criteria

Indications identified by NDT may be relevant or non-relevant depending on their dimensions, shape and location, as defined by the manufacturer.

- Relevant Surface Indications: Relevant surface indications are indications that result from discontinuities within the component. Relevant indications are considered those with major dimensions greater than 1/16 inch (1.6 mm) and associated with a surface rupture.
- Non-Relevant Surface Indications: Non-relevant indications are inherent indications not associated with a surface rupture that result from excessive magnetizing current, structural design or permeability variances in the component. Any indication believed to be non-relevant shall be re-examined to determine whether an actual defect exists. If magnetic particle indications greater than 1/16 inch are believed to be non-relevant, they shall either be examined by the liquid penetrant method to confirm they are non-relevant or they shall be removed and re-inspected to confirm they are non-relevant.

### 7.31.4 Assembly Requirements

All warning, certification, and informational plates or signs shall be installed per the manufacturer's requirements for safe use.

123

c. Place a 2-inch wide (±1/4 inch) white paint band around an acceptable component. The paint band should be 6 inches ±1 inch from the pin shoulder. The paint band should be 12 inches ±2 inches from the box shoulder for box × box components. Using a permanent paint marker on the outer surface of the tool, write or stencil the applicable DS-1 qualification class, the date, and the name of the company performing the inspection.

## 7.10 Specific Requirements for Shop Qualification of Fishing Tools

Foreword: In the Fifth Edition of Standard DS-1, inspection and qualification of fishing tools is specified in both Volumes 3 and 4. Volume 3 addresses the inspection of fishing tools that consist of a single piece with no component pieces (except welded sections). Examples include washpipe, junk mills, etc. Volume 4, first published with Fourth Edition, addresses fishing tools that are assembled from two or more components or sub-tools. Examples of such fishing tools include overshots, spear pack-off, etc.

## 7.10.1 Scope and Objective

This section provides additional specific requirements for shop inspection of fishing tools that are assembled from two or more components or sub-tools. The inspections outlined in this procedure are intended to help ensure structural soundness of the fishing tools. This procedure does not address functionality or wear resistance of the tools.

Compared to most other drill stem components covered by this standard, fishing tools are unique in several respects:

a. They come in a wide variety of geometric and mechanical configurations.

b. They often incorporate welded-on, pinned-on, or bolted-on subcomponents and parts that are subject to very high operating stresses.

c. Welded and brazed hardsurfaced areas are quite common on fishing tools and subcomponents.

d. Tools are often developed for specialty applications and may not be covered by industry-wide material or manufacturing standards.

Because of their nature, it is not possible to develop a simple written procedure that will foresee all configurations and answer all questions that might arise during fishing tool inspection. However, every effort has been made to make this procedure applicable to the widest variety of tools, and in most cases the procedure will adequately address the inspector's needs. If the instructions are clear, the inspector is required to follow them explicitly. However, because of the variety and complexity of fishing tools, the inspector may sometimes be faced with an accept/reject decision that is not clearly spelled out by this procedure. Should this occur, the inspector must advise the customer, manufacturer, or vendor of the tool (as applicable) giving the particulars of the situation, and the customer, manufacturer, or vendor of the tool (as applicable) will decide whether or not the tool is acceptable for use.

## 7.10.2 Definitions

The following definitions will apply in this procedure.

### 7.10.2.1 Acceptance Criteria

The attributes of a used component that make it acceptable (or unacceptable) for reassembly into a specialty tool or sub-tool. Acceptance criteria are tightest in structural base metal and weld metal, and least tight in hardsurfacing metal. Acceptance criteria for non-structural base metal are intermediate between these two extremes.

### 7.10.2.2 Fishing Tool Incidental Components

Fishing tool incidental components are fishing tool components such as springs, washers, bolts, screws, pins, nuts, seals, grapples, and the like, that do not meet one of the definitions under 7.10.2.3, Metals.

### 7.10.2.3 Metals

Metals in this procedure are classified according to their use in a particular fishing tool. Five different classifications are recognized:

a. Base Metal (Structural). A portion of the tool which, if it fails, could result in string separation or loss of all or a significant part of a pinned-on or bolted-on component. Structural base metal specifically encompasses all metal meeting the following tests:

- All metal located inside a projection of an imaginary cylinder encircling the end connection or connections (Figure 7.1). If two end connections on a tool have different outside diameters, or if the tool has only one end connection and a body outside diameter that is different from the end connection outside diameter, two imaginary cylinders shall be projected to establish structural base metal (Figure 7.2).

# 7.32 Specific Requirements for Shop Maintenance of Rotary Table Master Bushings and Bowls

## 7.32.1 Scope

This section provides additional specific requirements for the shop inspection of rotary table master bushings and bowls.

## 7.32.2 Preparation

The following steps must be performed to prepare for the inspection of the bushings and bowls:

### 7.32.2.1 Verification of Customer Requirements

Determine from the customer the required master bushing sizes. If any of the equipment properties do not meet the customer requirements, do not proceed with the inspection; notify the customer.

### 7.32.2.2 Application of Alerts

The inspector shall review any product bulletins and safety alerts from the manufacturer for the tool inspected. All modifications required shall be completed prior to final inspection.

### 7.32.2.3 Preparation of Surfaces for Inspection

a. Critical area maps for each component shall be provided by the vendor to the inspector so that the proper inspection areas can be prepared for inspection. If critical area maps are not provided by the vendor, the entire tool shall be treated as critical and inspected on 100% of its area.

b. All foreign material such as paints, coatings, dirt, grease, oil, scale, etc shall be removed from the critical areas prior to inspection. A form of grit-blasting is the preferred method for preparing equipment surfaces for inspection. However, other methods capable of completely removing the foreign material from the critical areas can be used, such as wire brush wheels, soft buffing pads, steam-cleaning, and paint-stripping.

## 7.32.3 Inspection Requirements

The Inspection Program developed by the vendor for the inspection of master bushings must include the following procedural requirements and common inspection methods:

### 7.32.3.1 Apparatus

The following equipment must be available for inspection: paint marker, metal scale, tape measure, and spring caliper. All apparatus requirements specified in the applicable common inspection methods are also required.

### 7.32.3.2 Traceability

Record the serial number and tool description. Reject the tool or assembly if no serial number can be located unless customer waives this requirement.

### 7.32.3.3 Common Inspection Methods Required

- MPI Body Inspection (7.19) with coverage as identified in 7.32.3.6.
- Liquid Penetrant Inspection (7.18) if necessary for proving up indications found using MPI Body Inspection.

### 7.32.3.4 Visual Inspection

Visually inspect the surfaces of the components to ensure no damages are present in manufacturer-defined critical areas of the master bushing and bowls, including the ID, taper, and pins. If damages are present, evaluate with the manufacturer's acceptance criteria. Unacceptable wear or damage (as defined by the manufacturer's acceptance criteria) in a critical area is cause for rejection.

### 7.32.3.5 Dimensional Inspection

In addition to the vendor's specified dimensional inspections, the following dimensional wear checks are also required for shop qualification of a master bushing. The dimensions listed below shall be measured, recorded, and compared to the manufacturer's allowable tolerances:

a. Master bushing ID at the top, 0.016 inch of wear is the maximum allowable unless otherwise specified by the manufacturer's dimensional requirements.

b. Master bushing ID at the throat, 0.063 inch of wear is the maximum allowable unless otherwise specified by the manufacturer's dimensional requirements.

c. Pin holes will be measured to verify that they meet the manufacturer's dimensional requirements.

d. When applicable, measure the diameter of the throat of bowls to verify it meets the manufacturer's dimensional requirements.

### 7.32.3.6 MPI Body Inspection Coverage

When performing the MPI Body Inspection (section 7.19) common inspection method, the inspection shall

cover all of the critical areas as defined by the manufacturer's critical area maps as well as the pins over 100% of their area. If no critical area maps are available, the entire component shall be considered critical and inspected over its entire area. Recheck all critical areas with the magnetic field oriented perpendicular to the first field.

## 7.32.3.7 Acceptance Criteria

Evaluate visual inspection, dimensional inspection, and NDT results against manufacturer's acceptance criteria. Indications identified by NDT may be relevant or non-relevant depending on their dimensions, shape, and location, as defined by the manufacturer.

- Relevant Surface Indications: Relevant surface indications are indications that result from discontinuities within the test part. Relevant indications are considered those with major dimensions greater than 1/16 inch (1.6 mm) and associated with a surface rupture.
- Non-Relevant Surface Indications: Non-relevant indications are indications not associated with a surface rupture that result from excessive magnetizing current, structural design, or permeability variances in the test parts. Any indication believed to be non-relevant shall be re-examined to determine whether an actual defect exists. If magnetic particle indications greater than 1/16 inch (1.6 mm) are believed to be non-relevant, they shall either be examined by the liquid penetrant method to confirm they are non-relevant or they shall be removed and re-inspected to confirm they are non-relevant.

## 7.32.4 Assembly Requirements

All warning, certification, and informational plates or signs shall be installed per the manufacturer's requirements for safe use.

## 7.32.5 Fit Testing Requirements

a. Master bushings shall be fit tested by completely assembling all components including installation of pins. Equipment may be disassembled for shipping purposes after a successful fit test has been performed.
b. Bowls shall be fit tested by installing them into an appropriate master bushing. It is the responsibility of the equipment provider to furnish an appropriate master bushing for testing.

## 7.33 Specific Requirements for Shop Maintenance of Rotary Slips

### 7.33.1 Scope

This section provides additional specific requirements for the shop inspection of rotary slips.

### 7.33.2 Preparation

The following steps must be performed to prepare for the inspection of the slips:

#### 7.33.2.1 Verification of Customer Requirements

Determine from the customer the required rotary slip sizes. If any of the equipment properties do not meet the customer requirements, do not proceed with the inspection; notify the customer.

#### 7.33.2.2 Application of Alerts

The inspector shall review any product bulletins and safety alerts from the manufacturer for the tool inspected. All modifications required shall be completed prior to final inspection.

#### 7.33.2.3 Disassembly

When performing maintenance on the tools to maintenance classification A1, the tool must be disassembled to the extent necessary that the manufacturer-defined critical areas of the load-bearing components can be thoroughly inspected.

#### 7.33.2.4 Preparation of Surfaces for Inspection

a. Critical area maps for each component shall be provided by the vendor to the inspector so that the proper inspection areas can be prepared for inspection. If critical area maps are not provided by the vendor, the entire tool shall be treated as critical and inspected on 100% of its area.
b. All foreign material such as paints, coatings, dirt, grease, oil, scale, etc shall be removed from the critical areas prior to inspection. A form of grit-blasting is the preferred method for preparing equipment surfaces for inspection. However, other methods capable of completely removing the foreign material from the critical areas can be used, such as wire brush wheels, soft buffing pads, steam-cleaning, and paint-stripping.

# 7.33.3 Inspection Requirements

The Inspection Program developed by the vendor for inspection of rotary slips must include the following procedural requirements and common inspection methods:

## 7.33.3.1 Apparatus

The following equipment must be available for inspection: paint marker, metal scale, tape measure, spring caliper, and a ruler. All apparatus requirements specified in the applicable common inspection methods are also required.

## 7.33.3.2 Traceability

Record the serial number and tool description. Reject the tool if no serial number can be located unless customer waives this requirement.

## 7.33.3.3 Common Inspection Methods Required

- MPI Body Inspection (7.19) with coverage as identified in 7.33.3.6.
- Liquid Penetrant Inspection (7.18) if necessary for proving up indications found using MPI Body Inspection.

## 7.33.3.4 Visual Inspection

a. Slip Segment Bodies: Visually inspect the surfaces of the slip segment bodies to ensure no damages are present in the critical areas. Slip segments or carrier segments shall also be visually inspected for straightness by placing a straight-edge or scale on the tapered back of the segment, confirming that the straight-edge sits flush against the length of the taper and parallel to the axis of the segment. Any visible deformation shall be cause for rejection.
b. Slip Segment Hinges: Visually inspect the hinges and the hinge pins. If bending is present, evaluate according to the acceptance criteria provided by the manufacturer. Unacceptable damage (as defined by the manufacturer's acceptance criteria) on the critical area is a cause for rejection.
c. Slip Dies / Inserts: Visually inspect slip dies to ensure that all dies are the same type and size, and have the same orientation when installed in the tool. Dies with worn, chipped, mashed, or otherwise damaged teeth shall be replaced.

## 7.33.3.5 Dimensional Inspection

In addition to the vendor's specified dimensional inspections, the following dimensional wear checks are also required for shop maintenance of rotary slips. Dimensions listed below shall be measured, recorded, and compared to the manufacturer's allowable tolerances. Unacceptable wear (as defined by the manufacturer's acceptance criteria) on the critical area is a cause for rejection.

a. Slip contact length
b. Critical dimensions of body hinges and door (for powered slips), as defined by the manufacturer
c. Hinge pin diameters
d. Internal diameters of the guide plates

## 7.33.3.6 MPI Body Inspection Coverage

When performing the MPI Body Inspection (section 7.19) common inspection method, the inspection shall cover all of the critical areas as defined by the manufacturer's critical area maps. If no critical area maps are available, the entire component shall be considered critical and inspected over its entire area. The critical areas shall include all surfaces of any hinge pins, handles, and handle pins (excluding inserts, buttons, or cotter pins). Recheck all critical areas with the magnetic field oriented perpendicular to the first field.

## 7.33.3.7 Acceptance Criteria

Evaluate visual inspection, dimensional inspection, and NDT results against manufacturer's acceptance criteria. Indications identified by NDT may be relevant or non-relevant depending on their dimensions, shape, and location, as defined by the manufacturer.

- Relevant Surface Indications: Relevant surface indications are indications that result from discontinuities within the test part. Relevant indications are considered those with major dimensions greater than 1/16 inch (1.6 mm) and associated with a surface rupture.
- Non-Relevant Surface Indications: Non-relevant indications are indications not associated with a surface rupture that result from excessive magnetizing current, structural design, or permeability variances in the test parts. Any indication

believed to be non-relevant shall be re-examined to determine whether an actual defect exists. If magnetic particle indications greater than 1/16 inch (1.6 mm) are believed to be non-relevant, they shall either be examined by the liquid penetrant method to confirm they are non-relevant or they shall be removed and re-inspected to confirm they are non-relevant.

## 7.33.4 Assembly Requirements

The Assembly Procedure developed by the vendor for rotary slips must include the following items when applicable:

- Seals: Replace old O-rings and soft seals with new ones prior to reassembly.
- Bolts: Measure all bolts used in slips and slip inserts that are load bearing with a bolt sizing tool to confirm that they match the length specified in the bill of materials.
- Slip Die Inserts: Ensure the die slot and load shoulders (if applicable) are clean before installing new slip die inserts.
- Hinge Pins: Ensure that hinge pins are properly installed with hinge pin retainers to prevent pins from inadvertently dislodging during use.
- Greasing Systems: Grease points and fittings shall be properly installed and the manufacturer-recommended lubrication applied.
- Signage: All warning, certification, and informational plates or signs shall be installed per the manufacturer's requirements for safe use.

## 7.33.5 Function and Fit Testing Requirements

The function and fit Testing Procedure developed by the vendor for rotary slips must include the following items. Once the equipment has been fully assembled as per the vendor's procedures, a function test is required to ensure that the equipment is operating properly.

a. Rotary slip assemblies capable of pneumatic or hydraulic operation shall be hooked up to a power unit capable of applying the same operating conditions that will be applied to the equipment while being operated on the rig. The power slips shall be function tested for a minimum of three (3) consecutive cycles. All components must function freely and without excessive play.

b. The equipment shall be fit tested with a test mandrel that is the same size as the drill pipe or tubulars that will be handled. The OD of the test mandrel shall be measured with micrometers or calipers in at least 2 circumferential locations (90 degrees apart) at each end and in the middle and be within 3% of the nominal OD of the pipe size.

c. When fit testing hand slips, the vendor shall provide the appropriate sized bowls for the slip type and tubular size that will be tested.

d. Fit testing shall ensure that the slips dies make proper contact with the test mandrel and the slip segments sit down completely in their bowl or housing.

## 7.34 Specific Requirements for Shop Maintenance of Tubular Elevator Bodies for Drill Pipe and Casing

### 7.34.1 Scope

This section will provide additional specific requirements for shop inspection of tubular elevator bodies. Elevator inserts and bushings are covered separately in 7.35.

### 7.34.2 Preparation

The following steps must be performed to prepare for the inspection of the tubular elevators:

#### 7.34.2.1 Verification of Customer Requirements

Determine from the customer the required elevator bore size and tensile rating. If any of the equipment properties do not meet the customer requirements, do not proceed with the inspection; notify the customer.

#### 7.34.2.2 Application of Alerts

The inspector shall review any product bulletins and safety alerts from the manufacturer for the tool inspected. All modifications required shall be completed prior to final inspection.

#### 7.34.2.3 Preparation of Surfaces for Inspection

a. Critical area maps for each tool's components shall be provided by the vendor to the inspector so that the proper inspection areas can be prepared for inspection. If critical area maps are not provided by the vendor, the entire tool shall be treated as critical and inspected on 100% of its area.
